schools.summary: That the word of the LORD was precious in those days, with no open vision: 'and the child Samuel ministered unto the LORD before Eli. And the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision' — the famine of the word — 'and the child Samuel ministered unto the LORD before Eli. And the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision': in those dark days of Eli's failing priesthood, the word of God was rare and prophetic vision scarce (the witnesses noting the significance — the spiritual barrenness of the time, that the word of the LORD was 'precious' (that is, rare, scarce, costly because so seldom heard), and there was 'no open vision,' no frequent or public prophecy spreading abroad, God withholding the light of his revelation in a season of corruption and declension, so that the very rarity of the word was both a token and a judgment of the low estate of religion, and a foil to the new dawn about to break in the call of Samuel, the dearth of the word a mark of the dark time, the scarcity of vision a withholding of God's light, the famine of revelation amid the corruption of the priesthood, the lesson — that the withdrawing or rarity of God's word is among the heaviest of judgments, a famine not of bread but of hearing the word of the LORD, 'behold, the days come... that I will send a famine in the land, not a famine of bread... but of hearing the words of the LORD,' Amos 8:11, the truth that where the word is rare the people perish for lack of vision, the light withheld in judgment, 'where there is no vision, the people perish,' Proverbs 29:18, the figure — the spiritual dearth that God in mercy breaks by raising up a new prophet, the night before the dawn, fulfilled when after long silence God speaks again, 'God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past... hath in these last days spoken unto us by his Son,' Hebrews 1:1-2, the truth that the preciousness of the word in a barren time should make God's people value and seek it, the figure of the famine of the word of God, the rarity of revelation in a time of declension, the call to prize the word of God and seek its return, the assurance that God breaks the silence in his time by raising up his messenger); the child Samuel ministering to the LORD under Eli, the one bright servant in the barren time (the servant amid the dearth, Amos 8:11) — the ministry (Samuel ministered); 'And the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision' (the word of the LORD rare and prophetic vision scarce, God withholding his light in a season of corruption, the dearth a token and judgment of the low estate of religion, the famine of hearing the word, the rarity of the word, Proverbs 29:18; Hebrews 1:1-2) — the figure (the spiritual dearth that God in mercy breaks by raising up a new prophet, the providence, the dearth, the call of Samuel to follow, vv.2-10) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'the word of the LORD was precious... no open vision' signify?

sides.position: The famine of the word of God, the rarity of revelation in a time of declension - the word rare and scarce, no frequent prophecy, God withholding his light in a season of corruption, the rarity a token and judgment of the low estate of religion, the withdrawing of God's word among the heaviest of judgments (Amos 8:11), the people perishing where there is no vision (Proverbs 29:18), the silence broken when God speaks by his Son (Hebrews 1:1-2)

themes: And the child Samuel ministered unto the LORD before Eli. And the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision.

the spiritual barrenness of the time, the word rare and scarce, no frequent or public prophecy, God withholding the light of his revelation in a season of corruption, the rarity both a token and a judgment of the low estate of religion

the withdrawing of God's word among the heaviest of judgments, a famine of hearing the word, the people perishing where there is no vision, the silence broken at last when God speaks by his Son (Amos 8:11; Proverbs 29:18; Hebrews 1:1-2)

schools.summary: Eli teaching Samuel to answer the LORD, Speak, LORD, for thy servant heareth: 'therefore Eli said unto Samuel, Go, lie down: and it shall be, if he call thee, that thou shalt say, Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth. So Samuel went and lay down in his place' — the posture of obedient hearing — 'therefore Eli said unto Samuel... if he call thee, that thou shalt say, Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth': Eli teaches Samuel the right answer to God's call, the readiness to hear and obey (the witnesses noting the significance — the model of the right reception of God's word, that the soul called of God should answer, 'Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth,' presenting itself as a servant ready to hear and to do whatever God shall say, the reverent attention that owns God's right to command and the willing submission that waits upon his word, the ear open and the heart bowed before the speaking God, the soul as servant, not master, before God's word, the readiness to hear before knowing what is said, the obedience that precedes the command, the lesson — that the right posture of the soul toward God's word is that of the listening, obedient servant, 'Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth,' the ear inclined and the will surrendered, 'the LORD God hath opened mine ear, and I was not rebellious,' Isaiah 50:5, the truth that we must hear God's word not as critics or masters but as servants ready to obey, 'be swift to hear,' and 'be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only,' James 1:19,22, the figure — the attentive and submissive hearing that receives the word to do it, fulfilled in the disciple who sits at the Master's feet to hear his word, 'Mary... sat at Jesus' feet, and heard his word,' Luke 10:39, the truth that to hear aright is to be ready to obey, the servant's ear open to the Master's voice, the figure of the attentive and obedient heart that waits to hear and to do the word of God, the call to receive God's word as servants ready to obey, the assurance that God speaks to the soul that says, Speak, LORD); Eli teaching Samuel to receive the call rightly, directing the child how to answer the speaking God (the instruction to hear, Isaiah 50:5) — the teaching (if he call thee); 'that thou shalt say, Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth. So Samuel went and lay down in his place' (the answer Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth, the soul presenting itself as a servant ready to hear and do whatever God says, the reverent attention and willing submission, hearing the word to do it, the obedient hearing, James 1:19,22; Luke 10:39) — the figure (the attentive and submissive hearing that receives the word to do it, the providence, the answer, the LORD coming and Samuel responding to follow, vv.10) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: Eli's submission, that hearing the sentence he said, It is the LORD, let him do what seemeth him good: 'and Samuel told him every whit, and hid nothing from him. And he said, It is the LORD: let him do what seemeth him good' — the resignation to God's will — 'and Samuel told him every whit... And he said, It is the LORD: let him do what seemeth him good': Eli, hearing the dreadful sentence on his house, bows in submission to God (the witnesses noting the significance — the genuine and humble submission of Eli, who, learning the whole heavy word of judgment upon himself and his house, neither murmurs nor disputes nor seeks to evade, but owns the hand of God in it, 'It is the LORD,' and yields wholly to his righteous will, 'let him do what seemeth him good,' the resignation of faith that bows to the sovereign and just disposal of God even when it falls upon itself, the witnesses noting that this submission, though true and commendable, is yet the mark of a man whose weakness was always in lacking firmness, here turned to a holy acquiescence, the owning of God's hand in the judgment, the yielding without murmur to his will, the resignation that justifies God even in his stroke, the lesson — that the believer is to submit to God's will even in chastisement, owning his righteousness and yielding to his disposal, 'I was dumb, I opened not my mouth; because thou didst it,' Psalm 39:9, the truth that the right response to God's judgments is humble acquiescence, justifying God and bowing to his sovereign will, 'shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?' Genesis 18:25, the figure — the soul that resigns itself wholly to the will of God, fulfilled in him who said 'not my will, but thine, be done,' Luke 22:42, the truth that to say 'It is the LORD' is to silence every murmur and rest in his righteous disposal, the figure of the faith that submits without murmuring to the righteous will of God, even in judgment, the call to bow to God's will in every stroke, the assurance that the soul which says 'let him do what seemeth good' rests in a righteous God); Samuel faithfully declaring the whole word, hiding nothing, the young prophet delivering the hard message in full (the whole word told, Psalm 39:9) — the telling (told him every whit); 'And he said, It is the LORD: let him do what seemeth him good' (Eli owning the hand of God in the judgment and yielding wholly to his righteous will, the resignation of faith that bows to God's just disposal even upon itself, justifying God in his stroke, the resignation to God's will, Genesis 18:25; Luke 22:42) — the figure (the soul that resigns itself wholly to the will of God, the providence, the submission, Samuel growing as a prophet to follow, vv.19-21) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Eli's 'It is the LORD: let him do what seemeth him good' express?

sides.position: The faith that submits without murmuring to the righteous will of God, even in judgment - Eli owning the hand of God in the judgment and yielding wholly to his righteous will, neither murmuring nor disputing, the resignation of faith that bows to God's just disposal even upon itself, the believer submitting and justifying God's righteousness (Psalm 39:9), bowing to the Judge of all the earth who does right (Genesis 18:25), resigning itself as Christ did (Luke 22:42)

themes: And Samuel told him every whit, and hid nothing from him. And he said, It is the LORD: let him do what seemeth him good.

Eli's submission, that hearing the sentence he said, It is the LORD, let him do what seemeth him good (the crux of the faith that submits without murmuring to the righteous will of God, even in judgment)

the genuine and humble submission of Eli, who, learning the whole heavy judgment upon himself and his house, neither murmurs nor disputes but owns the hand of God and yields wholly to his righteous will

the believer submitting to God's will even in chastisement and justifying his righteousness, bowing to him who is the Judge of all the earth and does right, the soul that resigns itself wholly to God's will as Christ did (Psalm 39:9; Genesis 18:25; Luke 22:42)

schools.summary: Samuel growing, the LORD with him, fulfilling all his words: 'and Samuel grew, and the LORD was with him, and did let none of his words fall to the ground' — Samuel grew under God's presence, and all his prophetic words came true (the and Samuel grew, and the LORD was with him, the LORD with him, the and did let none of his words fall to the ground, his words fulfilled, the figure, the prophet confirmed by the fulfilment of his every word, the LORD with him, the truth that God fulfilled all Samuel's words, confirming him a true prophet, Isaiah 44:26, the figure of the true prophet whose words God establishes, the growth of Samuel, the words fulfilled, vv.11); the LORD with Samuel (the favour, Isaiah 44:26) — the presence (the LORD was with him); 'did let none of his words fall to the ground' (God fulfilling all Samuel's words, the confirmation, the figure, the true prophet whose words God establishes, the providence, the establishment, all Israel knowing Samuel a prophet to follow, vv.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Samuel grew, and the LORD was with him, and did let none of his words fall to the ground.
Samuel growing, the LORD with him, fulfilling all his words
Samuel growing under God's presence, all his prophetic words coming true
the true prophet whose words God establishes (Isaiah 44:26)

schools.summary: That all Israel knew Samuel was established to be a prophet of the LORD: 'and all Israel from Dan even to Beersheba knew that Samuel was established to be a prophet of the LORD' — the prophet confirmed and the word restored — 'and all Israel from Dan even to Beersheba knew that Samuel was established to be a prophet of the LORD': the whole nation recognized Samuel as God's accredited prophet (the witnesses noting the significance — the great turning-point after the famine of the word, that God, having been silent and his vision rare, now raised up Samuel and confirmed him before all Israel as a true and established prophet of the LORD, the whole nation from north to south owning him as God's accredited mouthpiece, the dawn of a new era of revelation and the restoring of the word that had been precious, the confirmation rested on God's being with him and fulfilling his words, so that the people knew by the sure mark of fulfilled prophecy that Samuel was sent of God, the prophet raised up after the dearth, the word restored to Israel, the nation owning God's accredited messenger, the lesson — that God does not leave his people without a witness, but raises up and confirms his prophets and ministers to declare his word, 'I will raise them up a Prophet from among their brethren... and will put my words in his mouth,' Deuteronomy 18:18, the truth that God accredits his true messengers by his presence and the fulfilment of his word, so that his people may know them, 'when the word of the prophet shall come to pass, then shall the prophet be known,' Jeremiah 28:9, the figure — the faithful prophet God raises up to speak his word, fulfilled at last in the great Prophet, Christ, to whom all the prophets pointed, 'this is of a truth that prophet that should come into the world,' John 6:14, the truth that the restoring of the word is God's mercy to a people who had lost it, the figure of God raising up and confirming his prophet, restoring the word after the long silence, the call to receive God's accredited messengers, the assurance that God will always raise up a witness to his word); all Israel, from the farthest north to the farthest south, owning Samuel, the whole nation recognizing him (the nation's knowledge, Deuteronomy 18:18) — the recognition (from Dan to Beersheba); 'knew that Samuel was established to be a prophet of the LORD' (Samuel confirmed before all Israel as a true prophet of the LORD, the word restored after the famine, God accrediting his messenger by the fulfilment of his word, the great Prophet foreshadowed, the prophet established, Jeremiah 28:9; John 6:14) — the figure (the faithful prophet God raises up to speak his word, fulfilled in Christ, the providence, the establishment, the LORD appearing again at Shiloh to follow, vv.21) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Samuel's being established as a prophet signify?

sides.position: God raising up and confirming his prophet, restoring the word after the long silence - the turning-point after the famine of the word, God confirming Samuel before all Israel as a true prophet, the whole nation owning God's accredited mouthpiece, God not leaving his people without a witness (Deuteronomy 18:18), accrediting his messengers by the fulfilment of his word (Jeremiah 28:9), the faithful prophet fulfilled in the great Prophet Christ (John 6:14)

themes: And all Israel from Dan even to Beersheba knew that Samuel was established to be a prophet of the LORD.
That all Israel knew Samuel was established to be a prophet of the LORD (the crux of God raising up and confirming his prophet, restoring the word after the long silence)

the great turning-point after the famine of the word, that God now raised up Samuel and confirmed him before all Israel as a true and established prophet, the whole nation owning him as God's accredited mouthpiece, the dawn of a new era of revelation

God not leaving his people without a witness but raising up and confirming his prophets, accrediting his messengers by the fulfilment of his word, the faithful prophet fulfilled at last in the great Prophet Christ (Deuteronomy 18:18; Jeremiah 28:9; John 6:14)

schools.summary: The LORD appearing again at Shiloh, revealing himself to Samuel by his word: 'and the LORD appeared again in Shiloh: for the LORD revealed himself to Samuel in Shiloh by the word of the LORD' — God's revelation, long withheld, returned through Samuel at Shiloh (the and the LORD appeared again in Shiloh, the LORD appeared again, the for the LORD revealed himself to Samuel in Shiloh by the word of the LORD, revealed by his word, the figure, the renewed revelation of God after the dearth, the word returning to Israel, the truth that God revealed himself again through Samuel, the famine of the word ended, Amos 8:11, the figure of the word of God restored to his people through his prophet, the renewed revelation, the word returned, vv.1); the LORD appearing again at Shiloh (the return, Amos 8:11) — the appearing (again in Shiloh); 'the LORD revealed himself to Samuel in Shiloh by the word of the LORD' (God revealing himself through Samuel by his word, the renewed revelation, the figure, the word of God restored to his people through his prophet, the providence, the revelation, the word coming to all Israel and the war with the Philistines to follow, ch.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD appeared again in Shiloh: for the LORD revealed himself to Samuel in Shiloh by the word of the LORD.
The LORD appearing again at Shiloh, revealing himself to Samuel by his word
God's revelation, long withheld, returning through Samuel at Shiloh
the word of God restored to his people through his prophet (Amos 8:11)
